title: Form Theory: The Conceptual Architecture of Human Thought
description: Human thought takes place in a world that is uncertain and constantly changing. Yet the character of human thought is often quite different. People routinely simplify complex realities and hold their beliefs with a certainty and rigidity the world itself rarely warrants. The present paper develops Form Theory, a theory of conceptual structure according to which these recurring beliefs are surface signatures of a common underlying format. Form Theory proposes that each concept contains a central form: a default structure that encodes a compressed subset of the concept’s information, gives that information default influence within cognition, and activates strong default beliefs about the encoded information. I argue that this structure is not merely a source of distortion. By encoding selected information in a stable, certainty-generating, domain-neutral format, the central form makes rapid, complex thought possible, supports conceptual and linguistic creativity, helps establish an internal sense of identity, and enters crucially into the stable conceptual frameworks through which people organize their mental worlds. Form Theory characterizes the conceptual architecture of human thought: a default conceptual structure whose signatures are reflected throughout cognition, giving human thought much of its distinctive power, while also generating oversimplification, rigidity, and misplaced certainty.

title: Perceptions of exonerees based on exoneree gender and crime presence – A comparative analysis between United States and Australian community members
description: Wrongful convictions occur around the world, and exonerees (individuals who have been wrongfully convicted and have had their convictions overturned) experience stigma and discrimination by members of the public. However, most of the existing research on perceptions of exonerees has been conducted in North America, which may not generalize to other countries. The current research compared perceptions of exonerees held by members of the public in the US and Australia. It investigated the impact of exoneree gender and crime presence on perceptions of exonerees. US (N = 203) and Australian (N = 205) participants read a fictitious newspaper article depicting a man or woman who had been wrongfully convicted of murdering their child by setting fire to their home. The case facts were further manipulated, such that some participants read that afterwards it was established someone else had committed the arson (“crime” condition), while others read that the fire was caused by an electrical fault (“no crime” condition). Participants rated the exoneree on several dimensions, such as their culpability, characteristics (e.g., warmth, competence, aggression), emotions felt towards them, and participants’ desired closeness/distance to the exoneree. Across most measures, Australian participants perceived the exoneree more negatively than the US sample; gender and crime presence did not play a role in how the exoneree was perceived. The findings highlight that stigma towards exonerees can vary across jurisdictions, suggesting tailored education and intervention campaigns may be needed to best support exonerees.

title: How ball juggling training affects executive function, across the lifespan: Narrative review
description:
ABSTRACT
Background:
Juggling is a complex coordinative skill integrating visuospatial processing, prediction, bimanual control, and continuous performance monitoring. These characteristics make it a theoretically relevant motor–cognitive activity with potential implications for executive and related cognitive functions. However, it remains unclear whether physical ball-juggling practice leads to measurable cognitive benefits when assessed using standardized cognitive tests.
Objective:
To synthesize and critically evaluate available evidence on the effects of physical ball-juggling practice on cognitive function, with a primary focus on executive function, in healthy populations.
Methods:
A targeted narrative search was conducted in the Web of Science Core Collection. Eligible studies included empirical interventions involving physically performed ball juggling and reporting cognitive outcomes assessed with standardized performance-based tests. Given the sparsity and heterogeneity of the literature, a narrative synthesis approach was adopted.
Results:
Only three intervention studies met the inclusion criteria. Reported effects were modest and domain-specific, mainly involving visuospatial processing and attention-related measures. No study directly assessed executive function using comprehensive, domain-specific executive tasks.
Conclusions:
Despite strong theoretical plausibility, direct evidence supporting executive-function benefits of juggling practice is limited, reflecting under-investigation rather than clear absence of effects. Further well-designed studies are needed.

title: New Mothers and Fathers Show Distinct Emotion Dynamics across Maternity and Paternity leave
description: How do first-time parents regulate their own and each other’s emotions as they navigate maternity and paternity leave? Using experience sampling, we tracked Danish mothers and fathers’ emotion states six times daily for a week across maternity (N = 88) and paternity leave (N = 56). Bayesian multilevel coupled-oscillator models revealed distinct emotion regulation patterns that varied systematically with leave arrangements. Arousal amplified self-regulation dynamics specifically for parents not on leave, while co-located partners (verified through GPS) displayed emotional synchrony in both valence and arousal that disappeared in randomly paired couples. Mothers’ regulatory responses to fathers shifted across leave periods, weakly following fathers’ emotions during maternity leave but counterbalancing them during paternity leave, whereas fathers provided weak counterbalancing responses across both periods. These findings, combined with substantial couple-level variation, establish that couples function as mutually regulating systems where individual capacity and leave configurations jointly shape how first-time parents coordinate their emotional lives.

title: The Link Between Parental Education and Teenagers’ School Grades is Largely Genetic – Evidence from a Children-of-Twins Study
description: Why do children of more highly educated parents obtain better school grades? We addressed whether this reflects environmental effects of parents or familial factors shared across generations, using Norwegian population-register data (1.6 million individuals from 200k extended families) and genetically informative family designs. At age 16, teacher evaluations and national written exams in Norwegian (L1), English (L2), and mathematics were highly similar, with strong phenotypic correlations (r = .68-.84) and near-unit genetic correlations (r = .87-1.00). Associations between parental educational attainment and offspring grades were comparable across teacher evaluations, national exams, and grade point averages (r = .28-.36). For grade point averages, children-of-twins-and-siblings modelling indicated that most parent-offspring resemblance reflected genetic transmission (66%), alongside significant contributions from extended-family (25%) and parental transmission (10%). These findings suggest that intergenerational stability of education largely reflects genetic and extended-family transmission rather than teacher bias or direct effects of parental education alone.

title: Shared and Distinct Structural Features of Autism-Spectrum Traits and Personality Pathology: A Dimensional Approach
description: Current evidence suggests overlap between autism-spectrum traits and personality pathology. However, previous studies have mostly relied on categorical models, whereas dimensional approaches are increasingly emphasized in contemporary frameworks of psychopathology. The current study examined shared and distinct structural features of autism-spectrum traits, pathological personality traits, and borderline personality traits in a non-clinical sample of 704 adults. Participants completed self-report measures of these constructs. We estimated confirmatory and exploratory factor models, including bifactor models, to distinguish general from specific personality pathology. Autism-spectrum traits were substantially associated with pathological and borderline personality traits, although associations differed across autism-spectrum trait domains. The communication and reciprocity domain showed the strongest associations across personality pathology domains, followed by social interaction and spontaneity, while the imagination and creativity domain showed rather modest associations. Confirmatory bifactor models indicated that autism-spectrum traits were mainly associated with general personality pathology. After accounting for general personality pathology, detachment was the only specific personality pathology domain that remained substantially associated with autism-spectrum traits, particularly social interaction and spontaneity. Exploratory models largely confirmed these findings. Results suggest that autism-spectrum traits related to social communication and interaction overlap mainly with general personality pathology but also with detachment as a specific personality pathology domain.

title: Infants Use Physical Size, but Not Prior Possession, to Predict Conflict Outcomes, and Do Not Prefer Prior Possessors over Contenders
description: Ownership is a pervasive feature of human social life, yet its developmental origins remain unknown. One influential proposal is that human ownership psychology is built on an early-developing and evolutionary ancient sensitivity to first possession: Across species, conflicts over resources are often settled in favor of whoever held a resource first. Among humans, individuals who take what others already possess are often devalued as cooperative partners. Here, we focused on the inferences and preferences infants and toddlers form based on first possession. Across six laboratory experiments (N = 227), we found positive Bayesian evidence that 10–18-month-old infants and young toddlers do not expect intruders will defer to first possessors in territorial conflict, and that 10–13-month-old infants do not prefer prior possessors over those who take (or attempt to take) possessions from others by force. In contrast, we found that infants do expect that larger, more physically formidable agents will prevail in territorial conflict, using identical methods. Hence, prior possession alone is not sufficient for shaping infants' expectations and evaluations in dyadic conflict, though physical formidability is.

title: Visual Network tool: Individual belief networks from LLM-guided interviews and a visual canvas
description: Belief systems are theorized on individual and collective levels, but are typically studied and inferred only at the collective level. We introduce a new tool to measure individual-level belief networks from open-ended conversations, using a visual canvas. This ``Visual Network'' tool addresses several limitations of existing measures and enables fast and scalable measurement of the differences in content and structure of individual networks. Participants complete a semi-structured interview about their beliefs and behaviors on a given issue guided by an LLM, evaluate LLM-extracted statements that summarize the beliefs they have expressed, and construct personal belief networks by creating supporting and conflicting relations between these statements on a visual canvas. We validate the tool in a two-wave study on meat-eating. Participants evaluate the experience positively, and both belief network content and structure show satisfactory reliability across the two measurement points. We also find support for face, concurrent, and criterion validity of the measured individual belief networks. The new ``Visual Network'' tool opens up new empirical possibilities to test theories of belief dynamics and belief change.

title: Syntactic analysis provides nuanced insight into linguistic distancing’s relationship with momentary affect
description: Describing events as more psychologically distant – linguistic distancing – is an effective emotion regulatory strategy and is commonly measured using word-counting software that captures attention paid to dimensions such as the self (first-person singular pronouns) and the now (present tense verbs). Yet there is also evidence that sentence construction provides insight into how events are construed. Applying syntax-focused natural language processing to descriptions of everyday experience (2023-2024), we found that pronoun case mattered in addition to person and number – nominative “I” pronouns, which give the speaker an active role, carried more weight in predicting momentary affect. Likewise, verb aspect mattered in addition to tense – imperfective verbs, which describe activities as ongoing, were more predictive than perfective verbs. Specific features of syntax tied to construal can be used as a transparent and theoretically meaningful estimate of psychologically distanced language use, with the potential to expand understanding across languages and language use contexts.

title: Effectiveness of Early Alert Systems for College Student Success: A Systematic Review
description: This systematic review aimed to gather empirical evidence and evaluate if Early Alert Systems (EAS) support student success in higher education. We retrieved 28 empirical studies on EAS reported since 2010, focusing specifically on interventions aimed to enhance academic success among at-risk undergraduate students at four-year institutions in the United States. Despite the pervasive beliefs of their effectiveness, our review revealed no definitive support on the effectiveness of EAS which are currently implemented at various institutions. Majority of EAS often fail to fully integrate theoretical frameworks and rely predominantly on the efforts of professors and/or academic advisors, which points to the need for careful data-driven, theory-based system re-design, and collective institutional efforts for maximizing the effectiveness of EAS.

title: A comprehensive systematic review of participatory autism research over a 22-year period
description:
Background: Participatory research, which involves community members as partners rather than merely research subjects, has gained prominence in autism research. However, the extent and nature of participatory approaches across the field remain unclear.

Methods: We conducted a systematic review of autism research reporting community involvement, searching six databases. We coded who was involved, at what level, and in which research activities, and examined factors associated with participation.

Results: We identified 382 studies published between 2001 and 2023, with 95% published in the past decade. Two-thirds (67%) involved Autistic people, though one-third claiming to be "participatory" excluded them entirely. The most common participation level was consulting (31%), with community-led (5%) and community-controlled (1%) research remaining rare. Community involvement concentrated in study design (73%) rather than analysis (33%) or dissemination (29%). Participatory research was heavily concentrated in Western countries (USA, UK, Australia, Canada: 86%), applied research areas, and qualitative studies. Deeper participation was associated with identity-first language use and publication in journals with clear community-centered agendas.

Conclusions: While participatory language is increasingly becoming the norm in autism research, genuine power-sharing between researchers and community members is still a rare occurrence. Most participatory research operates at consultative levels, with academic researchers retaining primary decision-making authority. Moving toward authentic participation requires systemic changes to funding structures, institutional policies, and epistemic hierarchies that determine whose knowledge counts.

title: Immediate reward reduces procrastination in a naturalistic multi-day task
description: Procrastination is costly and pervasive, and delivering rewards immediately is a widely recommended intervention in self-help advice, yet this advice lacks experimental support. A key challenge in testing it is designing a paradigm that both mimics real-world environments where procrastination arises and permits experimental manipulation. Here we introduce the Multi-day Online Reading Task (MORT), which combines these properties and enables measurement of the full time course of work. In a preregistered experiment (n = 611), participants were given seven days to complete a self-paced reading task and received payment either immediately upon task completion or one day after the deadline. Immediate reward led all participants to start earlier and led those with high trait procrastination, who need help most, to complete work units and the overall task earlier across three reward-performance relationships. The benefits were mediated by motivation and did not come at the cost of task completion or persistence. These findings provide causal evidence that immediate reward reduces procrastination.

title: Understanding the Hidden Diversity of Socioeconomically Disadvantaged Adolescents: Developmental Pathways of Flourishing and Vulnerability
description: Socioeconomic disadvantage is often associated with poorer developmental outcomes, yet relatively little is known about why adolescents exposed to similar adversity follow markedly different developmental pathways. Drawing on data from the Singapore DREAMS longitudinal cohort (N =7,156), including 1,634 adolescents from the bottom 20% of the socioeconomic strata (B20), this study investigated developmental heterogeneity among socioeconomically disadvantaged students. Latent profile analysis conducted on adolescents with complete psychosocial indicators (n=611) identified five distinct developmental pathways ranging from Broad Psychosocial Vulnerability to Flourishing Despite Disadvantage. Differences in academic attainment and school performance across pathways were statistically significant but modest, whereas psychosocial differences were substantial, often exceeding one standard deviation. Profile membership predicted a wide range of subsequent flourishing outcomes, including academic engagement, academic self-concept, academic buoyancy, identity commitment, connectedness, optimism, and perseverance. Furthermore, flourishing adolescents from disadvantaged backgrounds demonstrated psychosocial functioning largely comparable to flourishing adolescents from more advantaged backgrounds despite occupying dramatically different socioeconomic positions. The findings challenge deficit-oriented accounts of disadvantage and suggest that human flourishing under adversity represents a distinct and measurable developmental phenomenon. Understanding the psychosocial conditions associated with flourishing may be as important as understanding the factors that place adolescents at risk.

title: The Development of Future-Self Concepts in Adolescence
description:
Mainstream media paints adolescents as moody, mean, and myopic in their decision-making and risk-taking, unable to act with their future selves in mind. Yet, adolescents may merely be sensing their future selves as distant and stranger-like, irrelevant to the goals and motivations of who they are now. This handbook chapter highlights the complex developmental processes through which young people form, refine, and prioritize their future selves. Drawing on a broad spectrum of interdisciplinary literature, we examine how adolescents aged 10 to 24 cultivate a
sense of continuity between their present and future selves, a key factor in long-term decision-making and overall well-being. Additionally, the chapter reviews innovative interventions, ranging from narrative exercises and role-play activities to immersive technologies like virtual reality and conversational AI, that help bridge the psychological gap between the present self and the future self. By synthesizing evidence from developmental psychology, neuroscience, human-computer interaction, and social psychology, it outlines promising directions for future research and practical applications aimed at empowering adolescents to make choices that promote sustained health, education, and financial stability.

title: Heartbeat perception is dissociable into subjective detection and temporal judgement
description: Heartbeat perception is widely studied as a behavioural index of interoception, yet different cardiac tasks share little variance, and what each task measures remains unclear. We developed the oneshot heartbeat detection task, in which each trial yields a subjective heartbeat detection response and, after a variable waiting interval, a cardiovisual synchrony judgement. As an exteroceptive control, the same trial structure was also implemented in a matched sound condition. We then asked how the two within-trial measures relate to each other and to conventional heartbeat counting scores obtained in the same sessions. Across three experiments (n = 43, 24, 21), subjective heartbeat detection on a given trial increased confidence in the synchrony judgement but not accuracy. Synchrony sensitivity remained stable across waiting intervals from 2 to 22 heartbeats. Cross-study analysis revealed that heartbeat counting performance was related to how frequently participants detected heartbeats and to their synchrony response criterion, but not to the synchrony sensitivity; in Experiment 1, the analogous indices in a matched sound condition showed no such relation. These data show that subjective detection and temporal judgement of heartbeats are dissociable components of heartbeat perception. We argue that weak convergence across cardiac paradigms may reflect this component structure rather than failures of measurement.

title: Koshering Psychedelics: Ayahuasca in the Ultra-Orthodox (Haredi) Jewish World
description: Background: Despite the rapid growth of psychedelic research, limited attention has been given to how psychedelics are used, experienced and integrated within highly conservative religious communities, and in Jewish contexts more broadly. This study addresses this gap by examining ayahuasca use among the ultra-Orthodox (Haredi) community, a highly religious, conservative, and collectivist Jewish group. Methods: This study used inductive thematic analysis of interviews with Haredi participants in Israel and the US (N = 23; age range: 21–60; 8 women), supplemented by participant observation and analysis of religious materials. Results: Six themes were identified: (a) settings: Haredi ayahuasca ceremonies were adapted to Jewish contexts; (b) motivations: use was primarily therapeutic; (c) acute ayahuasca experiences: experiences included distinct Jewish and Jewish mystical visionary content; (d) longer term religious effects: ayahuasca was linked to strengthened belief, connection to Judaism, and changes in religious practice; (e) religious tensions: included ayahuasca’s perceived foreignness to Haredi life, concerns about idolatry, mixed gender participation, and competing structures of authority (experience vs. text); and (f) strategies for addressing tensions: included medicalization, “koshering” ayahuasca’s set, setting, and experience (i.e., making them religiously permissible), and framing ceremonies as liminal spaces. Conclusions: This study broadens the scope of psychedelic science by examining ayahuasca use in a highly religious Orthodox context rarely included in the field. The findings show how ayahuasca settings, experiences, and long term effects are translated through Haredi and Jewish structures, involve religious tensions and resolutions, and highlight psychedelics’ contextual flexibility and continued diffusion into understudied populations.

title: There is only one correct analysis
description: Multimodel inference (such as multiverse analysis and many-analyst projects) is a family of sensitivity analyses that examines variation in results across alternative auxiliary assumptions of a research question. The observed variation in multimodel inferences has given rise to the idea that there is no single correct analysis, and that researchers should examine variability across a large range of plausible models instead. In this contribution, we critically reflect on the epistemological assumptions and consequences of this idea. Specifically, we argue that variation across alternative auxiliary assumptions can reveal uncertainty about how to validly test a hypothesis, but it cannot substitute for the epistemic warrant required to justify those assumptions. This is because, once a research question is translated into a specific empirical test by specifying the relevant auxiliary assumptions, there is a fact of the matter about which statistical analysis correctly implements that test and deviations from that analysis are not made epistemically acceptable merely by being included in a distribution. Thus, robustness across alternative sets of auxiliary assumptions with differing levels of justification does not, by itself, show that a theoretical claim is epistemically warranted. Researchers should invest in the empirical groundwork needed to identify valid tests of their hypotheses, rather than embracing variability across analyses and incorporating epistemic uncertainty into statistical inferences.

title: TRACKING MENTAL HEALTH AND COGNITIVE CHANGES FOLLOWING THE AUSTRALIAN SOCIAL MEDIA MINIMUM AGE LEGISLATION: A 4-WAVE LONGITUDINAL STUDY PROTOCOL
description:
Introduction: Over the last two decades, increases in adolescent mental health difficulties have been recorded alongside a rapid expansion in social media use. In response to these trends, on December 10, 2025, the Australian government implemented Social Media Minimum Age legislation prohibiting Australians under the age of 16 years from having social media accounts. The introduction of this legislation allows for a unique quasi-experimental investigation of the impact of social media use on adolescent mental health, and the mechanisms through which social media may impact young Australian adolescents’ mental health, wellbeing and cognition.

Methods and analysis: To assess the relationship between social media use and young adolescents’ mental health, wellbeing and cognition, a longitudinal, mixed-methods approach will be taken. 422 young people (aged 10-15 years) and parent/carer dyads were recruited and completed baseline assessment before the implementation of the Social Media Minimum Age legislation (October-December, 2025). Three follow-up assessments will then be completed after 6- (Apr-May 2026), 12- (Oct-Nov 2026) and 18-months (Apr-May 2027). Data from parent/carer and adolescent surveys, ecological momentary assessments (EMA), cognitive tasks and smartphone recorded screenshots of social media and messaging app use across each of these timepoints will be analysed. Linear regression analyses will be used to assess correlational relationships between social media experiences and responses and mental health outcomes, as well as how individual factors moderate these relationships. To assess these relationships over time, and between momentary experiences and responses (EMA data) and more stable trait-based (individual) and environmental (parenting and government restrictions) factors, we will use linear mixed-effects models and mixed-effects segmented regression.  

Ethics and dissemination: This study has been approved by the UNSW Human Research Ethics Comm

title: Is Democracy Worth the Effort? Seeing the Other Side as Willing Strengthens Pro-Democratic Motivation
description: Democracy takes effort, and these efforts do not always benefit one's own political side. Across two preregistered studies (total N = 916), we used computational modeling of effortful behavior to measure US partisans' motivation to strengthen democratic engagement in rural (traditionally Republican-leaning), urban (traditionally Democrat-leaning), or all US areas. Study 1 showed that participants were generally willing to make pro-democratic efforts, but less so when efforts benefited the out-group, particularly among more affectively polarized participants. We hypothesized that this asymmetry is sustained by misperceptions about out-partisans' willingness to engage in cross-partisan pro-democratic efforts. Study 2 showed that most participants (87%) underestimated out-party effort willingness. Importantly, correcting this misperception increased motivation to support out-group areas without reducing in-group motivation. These findings suggest that cross-partisan pro-democratic motivation is common but partly obscured by misperceptions about out-partisans' willingness. Making this willingness visible might help sustain the collective effort democracy depends on.

title: Early caregiving adversities and parental context effects on adolescents’ momentary emotion differentiation
description: Emotion differentiation—the ability to identify specific experienced emotions—associates with psychological health. Although theorized to develop through caregiving experiences, contextual effects on emotion differentiation in developmental populations have rarely been investigated, particularly among youth who have experienced disruptions in early caregiving. Here, we examined how adolescents’ momentary emotion differentiation varies across socioemotional contexts and whether these patterns differ following exposure to severe caregiving-related early adversities. Adolescents aged 10–17 with or without caregiving-related early adversities (N=81; mean age=13.49, SD = 1.97; 53% male/47% female, 9%-Asian American, 28%-black, 34%-white, 28%-other) completed 7-day ecological momentary assessment (4–5 surveys/day), reporting emotions and social contexts. At the trait level, there were no significant group differences in emotion differentiation. At the momentary level, negative momentary emotion differentiation decreased following negative parent-related contexts compared to other contexts. However, the momentary emotions’ standard deviation indicated also differentiated phasic intensification of a few negative emotions. Importantly, exposure to caregiving-related early adversities attenuated these contextual effects, L.Ratio(3) = 9.178 - 11.263. These findings provide initial evidence linking severe caregiving-related early adversities to affective dynamic processes and raise the hypothesis that early caregiving disruptions mitigate momentary parental modulation effects on emotion differentiation.

title: Touch of Loneliness Hurts the Most: The Implications of Loneliness and Social Touch
description: Loneliness is increasingly prevalent in emerging adults and is theorised to engage threat-related biology that overlaps with nociceptive processing. We tested whether social connectedness and related cognitive-affective traits predict endogenous pain inhibition, and whether hedonic appraisal of affective (C-tactile targeted) touch tracks these social variables or links to pain modulation. In February 2025, 119 healthy Generation Z participants (18-27 years; median age = 22) completed questionnaires indexing social network structure, contact quality, perceived social support, loneliness, self-concept clarity, early maladaptive schemas, depressive symptoms, and perceived stress. Participants then rated the pleasantness of standardised slow forearm brushing (~3 cm/s; 60 s), and underwent conditioned pain modulation (CPM) using pressure pain thresholds before and after a tonic cuff-conditioning stimulus. In the multivariable CPM model, higher loneliness was associated with weaker CPM (b = -0,90, 95% CI [-1,65, -0,15], p = 0,019) after adjustment for demographics, baseline pain threshold, stress, depression, self-concept clarity, perceived support, contact quality, and Young's schemas. Contrary to prediction, a schemas’ domain reflecting relational disconnection with affective inhibition showed a positive association with CPM (b = 11,28, 95% CI [2,91, 19,65], p = 0,009). Touch pleasantness was poorly explained by psychosocial variables; sex was the only reliable predictor (males lower; b = -0,90, 95% CI [-1,67, -0,13], p = 0,022). Touch pleasantness did not predict CPM in the model (p = 0,393). Overall, in healthy emerging adults, subjective loneliness, rather than perceived support or pleasantness of affective touch, was the most consistent psychosocial correlate of reduced endogenous pain inhibition.

title: Religious and Spiritual Experiences (RSEs) of Demons, Devils, and Satan: An Analysis of Features and Relationships to Psychopathology and Culture
description: This paper examines first-person experiences of demons, devils, and Satan contained in the Alister Hardy RERC database of religious and spiritual experiences (RSEs). Word frequency analysis, content analysis, and phenomenological analysis are used to characterize the features of demonic experiences and how they relate to psychopathology and religious/cultural affiliations. Results show that demonic experiences are less common than positively valenced RSEs in the RERC database, and that they disproportionately reflect psychotic-like experiences and mental health difficulties. Personal experiences of demonic forces also appear to be related to dreams and parasomnias (e.g., sleep paralysis), while reference to the possession/exorcism of other persons is associated with Pentecostal/evangelical affiliation. Results suggest that personal experiences of demonic forces are more associated with mental health difficulties than with particular religious or cultural affiliations within the current sample.

title: Handling Fully Irrelevant vs. Potentially Relevant Distractors as a Function of Perceptual Load
description: In a previous study, we investigated how task performance is affected by fully irrelevant distractors (sharing neither features nor location with the target) vs. potentially relevant distractors (sharing features and location with it), as a function of perceptual load. We observed a systematic dissociation: the effect of potentially relevant distractors increased under high-load, whereas the effect of fully irrelevant distractors remained unchanged. However, it is unclear whether this dissociation reflected a difference between distractor types or instead resulted from two methodological factors. The present study addressed this question by testing whether the double dissociation persists even when key experimental design aspects are controlled. Namely, (i) the effectiveness of the perceptual load manipulation, and (ii) the comparability between distractor effects. In Experiment 1, we replicated the perceptual load pattern and observed the double dissociation. The effect of fully irrelevant distractors decreased under high-load, whereas that of potentially relevant distractors increased. Three subsequent experiments systematically manipulated task parameters that differ between the original perceptual load task and our previous work, to assess their contribution to an effective load manipulation. Despite these variations, the double dissociation remained robust. In a final experiment, we further improved the comparability between the effects of each distractor type by using a common baseline measure, confirming that perceptual load modulates the effects of fully irrelevant vs. potentially relevant distractors in different ways. These findings suggest that distractor processing depends critically on its task relevance, while also highlighting  the need to investigate the boundary conditions of perceptual load effects.

title: Loneliness and Suicidal Ideation in the General Adult Population in Slovak Republic – Results from DISCONNECT study
description:
Background: Population-representative data on suicidal ideation and its social determinants remain scarce in Central and Eastern Europe; therefore, this study examined the association of loneliness and social isolation with suicidal ideation in a representative sample of Slovak adults.
Methods: Data for the present study were drawn from the DISCONNECT project, a population-based study that employed face-to-face interviews with a demographically representative sample of adults in the Slovak Republic (N = 3006). Suicidal ideation was assessed using item 9 of the Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9), alongside self-report measures of loneliness, social isolation, and depressive symptoms.
Results: Suicidal ideation was reported by 14.6% of participants (95% CI: 13.3–15.9) over the last two weeks. Depression emerged as the strongest predictor of suicidal ideation (OR = 21.22, 95% CI: 16.03–28.08, p < .001), while current treatment for a mental health condition was also independently associated with increased odds (OR = 2.07, 95% CI: 1.32–3.25, p = .001). Both loneliness (OR = 2.93, 95% CI: 2.21–3.88, p < .001) and social isolation (OR = 1.65, 95% CI: 1.22–2.23, p = .001) were independently associated with suicidal ideation, with loneliness showing the stronger association.
Conclusion: Loneliness was independently associated with suicidal ideation beyond depression and social isolation, highlighting subjective social disconnection as a potential target for suicide prevention strategies.

title: Regulating Self-Interest: The Effect of Anodal tDCS over the Right Dorsolateral 1 Prefrontal Cortex in the Ultimatum Game
description: In many social situations, a conflict exists between self-interest and social considerations such as fairness and reciprocity. Previous research suggests that self-interested behavior is an automatic response, requiring cognitive control to be regulated. Consistent with this view, neuroeconomics findings implicate the right dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (rDLPFC) in regulating self-interested behavior and implementing social preferences. Whereas prior neuromodulation studies have shown that inhibition or disruption of the rDLPFC increases self-interested behavior, studies examining the effects of enhancing rDLPFC activity have yielded inconsistent results. Moreover, it remains unclear whether such enhancement promotes responses to intentional unfairness alone or also to inequality per se. In the present study, we applied anodal transcranial direct-current stimulation (tDCS) over the rDLPFC to reexamine its causal role in regulating self-interested behavior among ultimatum game responders. In addition, we tested whether this manipulation promotes responses to both intentional unfairness and inequality resulting from an intention-free process. The results show that anodal stimulation increased rejection rates of unfair offers, regardless of whether they were proposed by a human partner or randomly generated by a computer. These findings suggest that rDLPFC activity supports the implementation of social preferences more broadly, promoting both reciprocal fairness and inequality aversion. When considered alongside prior findings demonstrating an opposite effect of anodal stimulation applied to an adjacent lateral prefrontal region using a similar paradigm, the results point to distinct and complementary roles of lateral prefrontal regions in managing the conflict between self-interest and social preferences. We discuss implications for dual-process accounts of social decision-making.

title: Individual differences and motives for the acceptance of cognitive enhancement: A mixed-methods investigation
description:
Despite a long history of human efforts to enhance cognitive abilities such as attention, memory, and intelligence, the proliferation of (new) technological and medical methods—including brain stimulation, pharmaceutical interventions, and gamified brain training—has renewed scientific and public interest in cognitive enhancement, even as evidence for their effectiveness in improving broader cognitive abilities remains inconsistent. Understanding who would be willing to enhance their cognitive abilities and why is crucial as these cognitive enhancement methods become more popular and accessible in society.
Thus, in two preregistered lab studies, we investigated individuals’ acceptance (i.e., hypothetical willingness to make use) of various cognitive enhancement methods, tested predictors thereof, and explored individuals’ motives to utilize cognitive enhancement methods or be reluctant to do so. 
In Study 1 (N = 203), we found considerably higher acceptance for more active (i.e., more involved) than passive cognitive enhancement methods. Lower age, greater interest in science fiction, and stronger investigative interests (partially) predicted participants’ acceptance of cognitive enhancement methods, while personality traits, self-assessed and measured intelligence did not.
In Study 2 (N = 197), we aimed to (conceptually) replicate key findings from Study 1, and in addition qualitatively assess the motives for the acceptance and rejection of cognitive enhancement methods. Our findings confirmed the higher acceptance of active than passive cognitive enhancement methods. Additionally, we found that greater interest in science fiction consistently predicted participants’ acceptance of cognitive enhancement, while personality traits and (self-assessed) intelligence did not show consistent associations. Critically, these quantitative effects were rather small. Qualitative analyses additionally provided exploratory insights into the motives for accepting or rejecting acti

title: Intentional Forest Exposure Scale: Initial development of two questionnaires
description:
Background and objectives
Most self-report measures of nature exposure have relied on a vague term “nature.” As forests offer humans various health-related and economic benefits, a more precise definition of “nature” in such measures to refer to forested areas would make the research focus more precise. This study therefore developed and preliminarily validated two measures (for adulthood and childhood): the Intentional Forest Exposure Scale (IFES) and the Retrospective Intentional Forest Exposure Scale (R-IFES), also addressing aspects of recreation and psychological restoration.

Methods
An online survey involving N = 99 university students’ responses (with 5,000 resamples bootstrapping) was used to explore the factor structure (using exploratory factor analysis; EFA) of IFES and R-IFES (initially designed to include six items each). These two questionnaires are identical in structure except the time frame: for IFES the last 12 months, and for R-IFES during childhood (5–10 years). Internal reliability and convergent, discriminant, and criterion validity were also tested. Face and construct validity were descriptively checked. 

Results
The EFA showed all six items loading onto a single factor for both IFES and R-IFES, but only one of the six R-IFES items yielded a poor loading of 0.39 (the loadings of the remaining 11 items were above 0.65). We therefore removed this R-IFES item and the corresponding IFES item, thereby keeping a parallel structure between the two measures. The five-item IFES and R-IFES were thus developed.
Internal validity was also high for both IFES and R-IFES, but convergent, discriminant, and criterion validity were weaker for R-IFES than for IFES. Additionally, R-IFES scores were more concentrated on higher values than IFES scores.

Conclusions
At least the five-item IFES would suffice to measure adult forest exposure. IFES can be used, e.g., in studies on forest recreation and pro-environmental behavior.

title: People make inferences from policy decisions regarding default options:  Replication and extension of McKenzie et al. (2006)
description: Default effect refers to the phenomenon that people tend to choose the default option in a choice-set. McKenzie et al. (2006) showed that one mechanism for the effect is that defaults are perceived as an implicit endorsement and a recommended option by the policy makers that set the defaults. In a pre-registered experiment with a Prolific online sample from the United States (N = 602), we conducted replications and extensions of Studies 1, 2, and 3 from McKenzie et al. (2006). We concluded a successful replication of all three studies, with support for their findings showing that: 1) people who are more willing to be organ donors are more likely to hold views that others should as well, and to think that enrollment should be the default, and that in turn: 2) people make inferences from policymaker’s set defaults (Study 2: organ donations; Study 3: retirement plans) regarding the policymakers’ own preferences regarding enrollment, and their views regarding whether others should enroll. In our extension we added a control condition with no defaults, finding that 1) people still make inferences about choice architects’ attitudes, even when defaults were not set, and 2) that setting defaults to not act (not enroll) is perceived as closer to no defaults than setting defaults to act (to enroll). Materials, data, and code are available on: https://doi.org/10.17605/OSF.IO/E5K4J.

title: Minimum and Maximum Values of Intraclass Correlations based on ANOVA in Two-way Designs
description: We study the range of the estimates of three types of intraclass correlation (ICC) in two-way designs: the ICC for absolute agreement of single measures (ICC(A, 1), the ICC for absolute agreement of average measures (ICC(A, K)), and the ICC for consistency of single measures (ICC(C, 1)). We use the ANOVA-estimates, as implemented in many statistical packages. In a design of N subjects with K measures, the range of ICC(A, 1) is the interval from -1/(K-1-K/N) to 1. The range of ICC(A, K) is the union of the interval from minus infinity to 1 and the interval from N to infinity: (-∞,1]∪[N,∞). The range of ICC(A, 1) is the interval from -1/(K-1) to 1. We also study the relationship between ICC(A, 1), ICC(C, 1) and the Pearson correlation for K = 2. Under the usual ANOVA assumptions, if there are strong row effects but no column effects and K = 2, then the probability that ICC(A, 1) > ICC(C, 1) lies between 0.5 and 0.6827. Furthermore, we show that ICC(C, 1) for K = 2 is a function of the Pearson correlation and the two standard deviations, and that ICC(C, 1) does not exceed the Pearson correlation.
